steps.title: AIG: 4 ANDs, two POs both compute a·b·c
steps.narrative:
Source: fraig_eq.blif writes the same function two ways: f1 = (a·b)·c and f2 = a·(b·c). Strash uses sorted-fanin keys, so (a,b) and (b,c) hash differently and both intermediates survive. The result: 4 structurally distinct ANDs computing the same logical 3-AND.

fraig (functionally reduced AIG) finds these by simulation + SAT and merges them.

panes.title: Per-node pipeline
panes.body:
1. process internal ANDs in topological order via Fraig_NodeAndCanon
  2. for each candidate: structural-hash lookup → folded if existing pair matches
  3. else: compute 32-pattern (per word) random simulation signature
  4. sim-table lookup by signature → if hit, candidate equivalence
  5. SAT-prove the miter — UNSAT confirms equivalence
  6. merge: candidate's pRepr = winner; later uses redirect

steps.title: Process ab — first AND in topo order
steps.narrative:
Fraig_NodeAndCanon(a, b): trivial cases (x·x = x, x·¬x = 0) miss; structural-hash table is empty; sim signature is computed from the random PI seeds.

Sim table empty ⇒ insert. No SAT call. ab survives as the representative of its sim class.

steps.title: Process f1 — sim signature is fresh
steps.narrative:
Fraig_NodeAndCanon(ab, c): structural-hash miss (no existing AND with fanins (ab, c)); sim is sim(ab) & sim(c) = 0x80808080. Not in the sim table.

Insert. f1 survives. The sim table now has two entries.

steps.title: Process bc — sim signature is fresh
steps.narrative:
Fraig_NodeAndCanon(b, c): structural miss; sim = 0xC0C0C0C0; sim-table miss; insert. bc survives.

Note that bc's sim differs from ab's even though both ANDs include b — the per-pattern bit-vector is keyed to the actual fanin combination, not just shared variables.

steps.title: Process f2 — sim HIT against f1!
steps.narrative:
Fraig_NodeAndCanon(a, bc): structural-hash misses (we have (ab, c), not (a, bc)). Sim is sim(a) & sim(bc) = 0xAAAAAAAA & 0xC0C0C0C0 = 0x80808080.

Sim-table lookup by 0x80808080 → hits f1. The pair (f1, f2) is a candidate equivalence. Sim equality on 32 patterns isn't proof (could be aliasing) — SAT verifies next.

steps.title: SAT verify the miter — UNSAT in 0 conflicts
steps.narrative:
Fraig_NodeIsEquivalent(f1, f2) builds the miter out = f1 ⊕ f2 in CNF (Tseitin: 8 clauses, 8 variables for this tiny cone) and calls Fraig_ManCheckClauseUsingSat with nBTLimit = 100 conflicts.

The solver propagates: if a node's value forces both f1 = 1 and f2 = 1 (or both = 0), the XOR can't be 1. Pure unit propagation closes it: UNSAT in 0 conflicts (trace reports propagations=9, inspects=15).

panes.body: If SAT had returned a counter-example, FRAIG would add the cex pattern to the dynamic sim words and re-simulate, refining future signatures so the false candidate doesn't recur. (Trace fields: Counter-example, Dynamic words.)

steps.title: Merge — f2 → f1; redirect PO_f2
steps.narrative:
Set f2->pRepr = f1 with output-complement bit 0. Subsequent fanouts of f2 (here just PO_f2) are redirected to f1. bc loses its only fanout (was f2) — it goes dangling, reclaimed by reference counting.

The aux pane shows the resulting AIG: 2 ANDs (ab, f1) with both POs sharing f1.

panes.body: With -c (or via dch), the SAT-proven equivalent f2 would be appended to f1->pNextE instead of discarded — the downstream if/map mapper then sees both structural alternatives per cone and picks the cheapest under its delay/area model.
